title:
Chatbot or Humanaut? How the Source of

Advice Impacts Behavior in One-shot Social

Dilemmas
description: {"blocks":[{"key":"5y6wq","text":"This paper explores how the source of advice (human vs. AI chatbot) impacts behavior in three classic social dilemmas. Utilizing a novel experimental design, we show that advice enhances welfare, but when it comes to prosociality, the source matters.  Players exhibited a preference for human advice over that from generative AI (genAI) and were more willing to pay for it. Prosocial behavior was more prevalent when players received human advice compared to genAI, increasing the probability of the cooperative stag hunt strategy and significantly driving contributions in public goods and dictator games.  Potential explanations include genAI's limitations in being truly empathetic and the possibility of moral wiggle room. Leveraging natural language processing (NLP) advances through large language models (LLMs) topic modeling, we demonstrate that the human and genAI advice differ significantly. Human advice was more objective, specific, intuitive, and norm-oriented, while AI advice tended to offer reasoning and targeted concepts of risk. We conclude that the adoption of genAI technologies should be balanced with an investment in human connectivity and organizational input with a goal of the constrained agency.  Preregistered at  Aspredicted.org #128933","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"w3k0j","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}}],"entityMap":{}}
steps: Our study design and hypotheses were pre-registered (Aspredicted.com #128933) and IRB-approved, #23-09. The primary investigation involves players’ preferences over the source of advice and how advice impacts prosocial behavior. To operationalize our inquiry, we conducted a 1x4 between-subjects experiment. Players interacted asynchronously (strategy method) in a one-shot stag hunt (to capture coordination/cooperation), public goods game/VCM with a contribution return rate α = 1.5 (to measure other-regarding behavior in the face of incentives to free ride, hereafter PGG), and a dictator game (a common measure of altruism). We presented these interactions in random order. Instructions framed the games as tasks but otherwise employed neutral language (details and payoff structures depicted in Figure 4, panel a) and omitted words like ‘cooperate’ or ‘free ride.’ Data were collected from workers on the Prolific.co platform. One upside to using Prolific is a pre-populated demographic profile for each subject, allowing us to minimize the number of questions in the instrument. Subjects earned a $1 participation fee and could earn bonuses up to $2.50 in virtual currency (tokens at the rate of US $1 = 100 tokens). Each game involved stakes of roughly 100 tokens, and the game paying final bonus earnings was randomly determined.
Subjects were randomly assigned to one of the following experimental conditions with comprehension checks.
Control (c1): Players played each interaction unadvised using the strategy method and composed around three sentences of advice to future players for each game.

Advice choice treatment (t1): Players played each interaction advised. Before committing to actions in each game, they chose whether to receive a randomly selected piece of advice from a human or a genAI chatbot.

Advice choice treatment with payments (t2): Players played each interaction advised. Before committing to actions in each game, they chose whether to receive between zero and three randomly selected pieces of advice from a human or a genAI chatbot. They paid a small fee following a price menu to receive the advice.

Random advice treatment (t3): Players played each interaction advised. Before committing to actions in each game, they were randomly allocated a piece of advice from a human or a genAI chatbot, but the source was not revealed to them. We incentivized them to guess the source.

Control subjects composed advice to be forwarded to treatment subjects. We used the Javascript editing features in Qualtrics to insert a corpus of human-composed advice to the treatment questions. Then, we set the balance counts for each condition to preload human-created content and ensure a flow of advice as requested. Thus, the instrument allowed randomization, compiled a sizable corpus of advice, and responded to subjects’ selections in real-time. While composing advice in the control condition required a certain amount of (compensated) effort, there was no incentive for strategic misguidance because the advice was not meant for the player’s counterpart [contrasting with advice in Sobel, 2013].

A corpus of AI-generated advice regarding the three games was preloaded into our instrument. Players assigned to the advice treatments were advised with genAI content from ChatGPT4 and Google Bard – LLMs we deemed the most capable after a comparison of several models on sdk.vercel.ai. While we assumed players had some familiarity with genAI chatbots, we provided the following definition (generated by ChatGPT4): “An AI chatbot is a computer program that uses artificial intelligence (AI) and natural language processing (NLP) to communicate with humans through chat interfaces, such as messaging apps, websites, or mobile apps. Chatbots (such as ChatGPT4 or Google Bard) can simulate human-like conversations and help users with various tasks, such as answering questions, providing information, or executing simple commands.” After playing the games and executing advice choices, all players provided their beliefs about existing injunctive norms through an incentivized coordination game [following Krupka and Weber, 2013], commented on their perception of social norms, and decided over risky lotteries [via the Eckel and Grossman, 2002, method].

A pre-collection power test suggested a minimum sample size of 140 observations for the advice-giving control and 230 per treatment. We ultimately included 147 subjects in the control and 247 in each of the three treatments. A subject was 32.26 years on average, and the sample was 50% female (both uniform across treatments); these demographics are consistent with most studies using samples of Internet-savvy subjects. Because the study involves attitudes toward technology, we confirmed no meaningful age differences across treatments using Kolmogorov-Smirnov tests. The ethnic background of the sample was 67.65% White, 13.55% Black, 5.92% Asian, 4.44% Hispanic or Other, and 8.31% Mixed. Thirty-three percent of the subjects were from the US, and the mean of 712 approvals suggests they were fairly experienced on Prolific. The average completion time was about nine minutes. Players’ final bonus payoffs were determined by randomly choosing one of the three games, lottery results, and incentivized coordination over norms. The mean compensation rate amounted to roughly US $9.25/hour, and the study paid out $3105.

The authors debated several potential approaches to compiling the advice corpora before settling on the following methods. We note that “prompt engineering” is as much an art as a science; a slight variation in a prompt can lead to drastically different results. While we were mindful to collect meaningful and authentic advice from both genAI and human subjects, we make no claims about the “correctness” of either form nor their consistency with theory (in fact, advice was often obtuse).

GenAI advice results from an LLM being trained on game theory and behavioral economics articles, among other inputs. We did not pre-screen or evaluate the advice for factual or theoretical correctness, nor did we suggest that we endorsed the advice they would receive. Adulterating or cherry-picking advice to send players in the treatments more closely approached deception than providing raw advice, warts and all. Following Schotter [2023, p. 1], advice does not have to derive from experts to be meaningful. Thus, we are confident that the advice corpora are suitable for capturing the effects we explore.

Previous studies propose that there is value in eliciting advice. Nyarko et al. [2006] set up a market for advice and allow subjects to bid for advice based on the expertise of the source; in their setup, advice is binary, whereas in ours, advice is free-form text.

Using two prompt styles, we elicited advice from ChatGPT4 and Google Bard on three games. We used neutral language and entered the subject instructions verbatim, avoiding game names. For practical genAI advice, specificity was required in prompts, such as equating the ‘stag’ strategy with ‘Blue’ in our context. An alternative approach would have been allowing subjects to create their prompts. However, we recognized that, despite understanding the games, they might lack the genAI experience to elicit useful responses, and individual prompts would render between-subject comparisons invalid.

The following text depicts our prompts to ChatGPT4 and Google Bard. The text is essentially the same instructions we gave human subjects; prompt engineering for genAI requires some clarifying statements and examples meant to exclude meaningless output.

Mega Prompt 1: Stag Hunt Suppose I will randomly match you with another participant in this task. You and your counterpart will be asked the same question. You will play once only and have no communication. You each have to decide between one of two strategies: Blue or Yellow. You will make this choice once and must choose without observing your counterpart’s choice. Anytime either of you chooses to Yellow, that person is guaranteed a payoff of 50 tokens regardless of what the other chooses. If each of you chooses Blue, each of you will receive a bonus payoff of 100 tokens. If either player chooses Blue when the other chooses Yellow, they receive no bonus payoff and zero tokens. The player choosing Yellow gets the guaranteed payoff of 50 tokens. In no fewer than three sentences and in your own words, please provide some thoughtful advice to a future player about how best to play in this interaction.

Mega Prompt 1: PGG Now, suppose I will randomly match you with another participant in this task. You and your counterpart will be asked the same question. Each of you will have 100 tokens. You each have to decide how many tokens to transfer to a public fund. All transfers will grow by 50%. Each player gets an equal share of the public fund. You play once, and there is no communication. Your final payoff will be the sum of 1) what you did not transfer plus 2) your share of the public fund. In no fewer than three sentences and in your own words, please provide some thoughtful advice to a future player about how best to play in the same interaction that you just had.

Mega Prompt 1: Dictator We will randomly match you with another participant in this task. You and your counterpart will be asked the same question. There is an endowment of 100 tokens among you. You play once, and there is no communication. There will be two roles: Role A and Role B. You will be randomly assigned one of the two roles. Role A decides how many tokens from the endowment to allocate to Role B. Role B has no action and must accept Role A’s allocation. Your final payoff will depend on the role assigned to you: If you are in Role A, your payoff is 100 tokens minus what you allocate to Role B, If you are in Role B, your payoff is what Role A allocates to you. In no fewer than three sentences and in your own words, please provide some thoughtful advice to a future player about how best to play in the same interaction that you just had. You will make this choice once in Role A and must choose without observing your counterpart’s choice.

The resulting corpus contains advice for each interaction, with prompts crafted from the instructions for usefulness and accuracy. Without specifying the one-shot nature of the games, genAI occasionally gave irrelevant advice on repeated interactions. We didn’t alter AI-generated content; we only formatted it into bullet points.

In the control condition, subjects composed advice for future players immediately after committing to action in each game but before recording their beliefs about existing norms (to avoid priming). The following text depicts our prompt to the subjects. Please be thoughtful, complete, and mind grammar in this part of the task. We will review all responses before compensation. AI-generated responses are not allowed. In no fewer than three sentences, more than 40 words. And in your own words, please provide some thoughtful advice to future player about how best to play in the same interaction that you just had. Note, you’re not writing to your counterpart.

Subjects composed whatever advice they saw fit; we pre-screened but did not edit or spell-check those responses before providing them to players in the treatment. To ensure data quality and protect participant anonymity, it may be necessary to exclude messages from a research study [see Brandts et al., 2019]. We removed nonsensical responses and identified potentially AI-generated content with GPTZero.com. We acknowledge that some players may have believed they were communicating with their counterparts. The resulting human corpus included eighty-nine pieces of advice for each interaction.

One significant upside of the method is a corpus large enough to conduct the text analysis key to understanding why sources of advice may have value to players. We conducted a deep dive into natural language processing to achieve this goal.

After an extensive literature review, we consider Gentzkow et al. [2019] the current standard reference for using text as economic data. Many papers have adopted text-based data to explore economic or social sciences problems [for example, Rubinstein, 1996, Eckel and Wilson, 2004a, Charness and Dufwenberg, 2006, Houser and Xiao, 2011, Chen and Chen, 2011, Rand et al., 2012, Lucas et al., 2015, Ding and Schotter, 2017, Saltzman and Yung, 2018, Tingley et al., 2018, Babin and Chauhan, 2023, Kalabikhina et al., 2023]. Historically, such text analysis in studies like ours has been limited to coding chat-logs/classification techniques, word frequency analysis, and dictionary-based ‘bag of words’ approaches.

Our analysis utilized the most sophisticated methods available to us at the time of writing. We recognize that the tools we reference in the following paragraphs are likely to be unfamiliar to most outside of the computer and information sciences; thus, we provide a brief description of each method accompanying our results. Our corpora were cleaned and preprocessed using the following Natural Language Processing (NLP) methods.

Tokenizing: Splitting text into smaller units (tokens), which can be words, phrases, or symbols, allowing algorithms to work with individual components of the text. For example, tokenizing the sentence "I enjoy analyzing text" might result in the tokens "I", "enjoy", "analyzing", and "text".

Stemming: This process involves reducing words to their base or root form. For example, the stemming process would convert words like "analyzing" and "analyze" into their stem analyz. This step makes it easier for algorithms to process text and humans to interpret results.

Stopwords: Some terms are commonly removed from the text before processing because they carry minimal meaningful information for analysis. Examples include "and", "the", and "is." Removing stopwords helps focus on the more significant words that carry the core meaning of the text; thus, the process involves some understanding of the corpora and trial and error. We removed words that are portions of the question used to elicit the response (e.g., "advice" and "interaction"). On a few rare occasions, we removed what we deemed as game-specific stopwords.
Vectorizing: This process involves transforming text data into a numerical vector format suitable for algorithmic analysis and pattern recognition.

Sentiment Analysis TextBlob sentiment analysis, as described by Loria [2018] and implemented in Python’s natural language toolkit (NLTK), was utilized for its simplicity and accessibility in processing and analyzing textual data to determine polarity (i.e., negative, neutral, or positive sentiment, on a -1 to 1 scale) and subjectivity (objective or subjective perspective, on a 0 to 1 scale). The terms are non-stemmed, and we applied no stopwords.

Term Weighting (TF-IDF) TF-IDF term weighting demonstrates significant differences in the relative informational importance of terms in advice by source – the salience of a term. We note that term/bigram frequency and ‘bag of words’ count models, [such as LIWC Tausczik and Pennebaker, 2010], have been largely replaced with the TF-IDF statistic – widely used in text mining, search engine scoring, and data preprocessing for machine learning models. TF-IDF is significantly more beneficial for document classification and clustering tasks. Still, it has limitations, such as being unable to capture context and semantics. The corpora terms are stemmed, and we applied custom stopwords.

Topic Modeling One of our goals is to expose the underlying themes and content of each piece of advice. Every document (a piece of advice) is a mixture of topics, as is a corpus. Our method, topic modeling, extracts conceptual topics from unstructured text data. At the time of Gentzkow et al. [2019], the most sophisticated topic modeling technique was Latent Dirichlet Allocation (LDA), a Bayesian framework to reveal underlying topical patterns in text data. We refer the reader to Jelodar et al. [2019] for a survey of LDA applications and Egger and Yu [2022] for a cost-benefit comparison of contemporary topic modeling techniques. We also employed more traditional methods, such as LDA, bigram frequency, and Kamada et al. [1989] co-occurrence network analysis for robustness. While LDA remains a fundamental technique, it has several limitations, such as assuming the independence of terms and being agnostic to textual context. Recently, sophisticated transformer-based models like Google’s BERT have been developed, which capture semantic relationships between s, words using the context-aware c-TF-IDF approach.

BERTopic [Grootendorst, 2022] addresses the downsides of a model like LDA by exploiting a pre-trained large language model framework and package modularity to generate topics to capture the intent and context of advice – what it talks about. BERTopic differs from LDA by using the context-focused c-TF-IDF algorithm to compare term importance within clusters and generate the optimal number of topic representations. Instead of treating a single term as a cluster’s centroid, it regards all documents within a cluster as unique. Topic labels are derived from keywords with the highest c-TF-IDF scores. These interpretations remain subjective and depend on the specific data context. The method classifies tokenized and vectorized parts of text into topic bins. The corpora terms are stemmed, and we applied game-specific custom stopwords.
